data.title: Warehouse REIT Lands 10-year Lease Renewal for British Property
data.description:
Warehouse REIT agreed to renew the lease for a 375,000-square-meter space at the Midpoint 18 property in Middlewich, UK, for logistics company Wincanton (WIN.L).

Under the renewed 10-year deal, the specialist warehouse investor will receive a new headline rent of 6.50 pounds sterling per square foot, representing an uplift of 28.8% from the previous rent, according to a Monday filing.

The renewal also covers an occupier break in the third and fifth years.

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0904 GMT - Bitcoin falls as cryptocurrencies come under pressure from U.S. regulatory uncertainty and widespread risk aversion, Zaye Capital Markets analyst Naeem Aslam says in a note. A crypto summit at the White House on Friday did not provide any details on President Trump's plans for regulation of the sector. Markets are also grappling with concerns over global trade tensions, deflationary pressures in China and the prospect of a weakening U.S. economy, Aslam says. Bitcoin falls 5.5% to $81,493 after hitting a one-week low of $80,085 overnight, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0840 GMT - The euro consolidates after last week's rally against the dollar as markets await news on German fiscal policy and Ukraine peace talks, ING analyst Chris Turner says in a note. A key area of focus is on whether German Chancellor-in-waiting Friedrich Merz can get his 500 billion-euro infrastructure fund through parliament, he says. He is in discussions with the Greens political party and the Bundestag lower house is expected to vote on the measures on March 18. "Headlines on whether the Greens are playing ball this week could trigger some volatility in the euro." The euro falls 0.2% to $1.0820 after hitting a four-month high of $1.0890 Friday, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0840 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds fall as investors expect more interest rate reductions from the Bank of England due to dwindling spending and growth. "Weak business and consumer sentiment may result in further rate cuts from the Bank of England," Lindsay James, investment strategist at Quilter Investors says in a note. The 10-year gilt yield falls 2 basis points to 4.637%, Tradeweb data shows. The 2-year gilt yield declines 1bp to 4.193%. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0815 GMT - India's consumer inflation is expected to have eased to 3.8% on year in February, according to the median estimate of 11 economists polled by The Wall Street Journal. That would compare with the 4.3% rise in January's CPI. Economists mostly expect broad-based declines in food and vegetable prices to drag inflation lower. Inflation is unlikely to rebound, giving the central bank room to ease rates to support the economy, Capital Economics' Shilan Shah and Joe Maher write in a note. "Weak domestic demand, higher crop output and muted manufacturing costs are likely to offset upside risks from a weak currency," Nomura research analysts say in a note. Both expect an additional 75 bps of rate cuts this year, which would bring the repo rate to 5.5% by the year's end. The inflation data are due Wednesday. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0814 GMT - There may be some hurdles for the Bank of Thailand to deliver a consecutive rate cut in April, Citi Research economists say in a note. The central bank had already factored in some of the risks associated with additional U.S. tariff hikes when delivering its surprise rate cut in February, they write. Citi lowers its 2025 and 2026 inflation forecasts to 0.9% and 1.0%, respectively, from 1.0% and 1.4%. Its 2025 inflation forecast is below the BOT's, reinforcing the risk of inflation undershooting the central bank's target for a second consecutive year. The pressure for another rate cut will persist, particularly as falling credit growth alleviates concerns about the financial stability costs of monetary easing, they say. Citi reiterates its expectation for another 25 bps cut in August.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0812 GMT - Eurozone 10-year government-bond yield spreads over German Bunds mostly narrow but should remain choppy amid European Union plans to ease debt rules, says Commerzbank Research's Rainer Guntermann in a note. "Eurozone government-bond spreads should also remain choppy as there could be more color about the EU's plan to relax debt rules from tonight's Eurogroup meeting," the rates strategist says. Germany is pushing for a permanent change of the EU's Stability and Growth Pact to exclude defense spending to avoid new joint debt, Guntermann says. The 10-year French OAT-German Bund yield spread falls 1.5 basis points to just below 72bps, while the 10-year Italian BTP-German Bund yield spread is flat at 111.5bps, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0806 GMT - The Canadian dollar could rise in the medium term if Canada's newly announced prime minister Mark Carney successfully counters U.S. tariffs, Swissquote Bank analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya says in a note. However, in the short-run the currency faces selling pressure as the Bank of Canada is expected to cut interest rates by another 25 basis points on Wednesday, she says. Carney was named prime minister after winning the Liberal Party leadership. The former governor of Bank of Canada and Bank of England succeeds Justin Trudeau, who announced in January that he would step down after his approval rating plummeted. Carney said he would keep retaliatory tariffs until "Americans show us respect." USD/CAD falls 0.1% to 1.4361. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0803 GMT - Germany's proposed increase in spending is likely to limit interest-rate cuts by the European Central Bank, ING analysts say in a note. The ECB is likely to keep interest rates on hold in April and make one cut before the summer, ING says. The planned additional spending is likely to boost economic growth, but it would take time for the positive effects to be seen, the analysts say. "Capital investment takes time to deploy; shovel-ready projects aren't always readily available." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0751 GMT - From a fair value perspective, ING rates strategists see strong reasons for the 10-year German Bund yield to rise towards 3%, with recent headlines and data points generally supporting higher yields, they say in a note. However, the tides could shift quickly, causing long-end government bond yields to fall faster than short-end bond yields--a phenomenon called "bull flattening," they say. "Any setbacks, be it in terms of actual spending, geopolitical tensions, or growth headwinds could easily bull flatten the curve again," the strategists say. The 10-year Bund yield last trades 2.2 bps higher at 2.853%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - The European Central Bank might be too pessimistic about the eurozone's growth chances this year as a planned spending drive looks set to boost activity, Pantheon Macroeconomics' Claus Vistesen and Melanie Debono write in a note to clients. The central bank last week cuts its estimates for output growth in the currency area, pointing notably to the risk of U.S. import tariffs. But those tariffs might not materialise in a sustained fashion, Vistesen and Debono says. And new plans in Germany and across the wider EU to spend more on infrastructure and defense offer a countervailing shot in the arm, they say. "[L]ast week's fiscal announcements point to more balanced risks further out," they say. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0737 GMT - Last week's historic selloff in German Bunds, which resulted from Germany's large-scale fiscal-loosening plans, "may not prelude a bear trend," Citi Research's strategists say in a note. While the market may want to test the 3% level for the 10-year Bund, the focus may soon shift to more immediate drivers, which lean bullish, including continuing European Central Bank rate cuts, they say. Thus, tentatively, Citi strategists' base case for the 10-year Bund yield is between 2.25% and 2.75% for the quarters ahead. The 10-year Bund yield rises 2.5 basis points to last trade at 2.856%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0737 GMT - The dollar trades steady but remains at weaker levels after losses last week amid uncertainty over U.S. President Trump's policies. The big issue for markets is the fact that Trump's policies change as "often as the direction of the wind does," Pepperstone strategist Michael Brown says in a note. Trump's whipsawing on tariffs makes it impossible for market participants to price risk or accurately discount a future policy path, he says. There is no surprise that "nobody wants to touch dollar denominated assets right now" with the DXY dollar index suffering its worst week in two-and-a-half years last week, he says. The DXY trades flat at 103.853 after hitting a four-month low of 103.458 Friday.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0905 GMT - Deliveroo's exit from Hong Kong feeds the long-term bear hypothesis that the British online food delivery company could be pushed out of some of its smaller markets by bigger, better-funded competitors, Panmure Liberum analyst Sean Kealy says. Deliveroo's Ebitda and gross-transaction-value growth rate are set to benefit from the group's exit from Hong Kong, which was one of its most discount-sensitive markets. Panmure Liberum reiterated its buy recommendation on the stock, saying that the company can generate a level of cash flow over the long term that is currently under-appreciated by the market. Shares are up 1.1% at 1.26 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0904 GMT - Bitcoin falls as cryptocurrencies come under pressure from U.S. regulatory uncertainty and widespread risk aversion, Zaye Capital Markets analyst Naeem Aslam says in a note. A crypto summit at the White House on Friday did not provide any details on President Trump's plans for regulation of the sector. Markets are also grappling with concerns over global trade tensions, deflationary pressures in China and the prospect of a weakening U.S. economy, Aslam says. Bitcoin falls 5.5% to $81,493 after hitting a one-week low of $80,085 overnight, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0903 GMT - Malaysia's benchmark Kuala Lumpur Composite Index closed 0.7% lower at 1536.46. The KLCI faces further downside amid weak technical momentum and concerns over U.S.-China trade tensions, TA Securities analyst Stephen Soo says in a note. However, persistent foreign selling may peak, supporting an oversold rebound, he reckons. Soo pegs the KLCI's support at 1529 and resistance at 1580. Among decliners, Sime Darby fell 3.3% and Axiata Group shed 3.2%. Meanwhile, Genting Bhd. added 2.5% and YTL Corp. was 1.1% higher. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0900 GMT - Thales's space business, which accounts for 11% of the French aerospace and defense group's revenue, continues to be a source of concern given the structurally weaker commercial telecoms market, Berenberg analysts say. The business generated a 65 million euros loss before interest and taxes in 2024, equivalent to a -3% margin. Thales expects this margin to improve to 7% in 2028. However, Berenberg forecasts the margin will reach just 5% in three years' time. Thales, Airbus and Leonardo are exploring the possibility of bringing together their space units. The best formula would be a joint-venture structure similar to that of the European missile consortium MBDA, which was formed by Airbus, BAE Systems and Leonardo, the Berenberg analysts say. They caution that this outcome seems challenging in the near term. Shares are up 1.4% at 239.9 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0846 GMT - Dassault Aviation's outlook for the Rafale fighter jets is solid, providing the bedrock for the investment case into the French aerospace and defense group, Berenberg analysts say. Dassault has indicated that the production rate of Rafale jets is increasing to four a month from three, and could rise to five aircraft a month if there are enough orders. The company is expecting an order for 26 Rafale from the Indian Navy imminently. "With a solid backlog of Rafale fighter jets and several opportunities for further sales in the coming years, the defense division backdrop remains healthy," the analysts wrote in a note to investors. "In the near to midterm, key opportunities include orders from India, France and Saudi Arabia." Berenberg raised its price target for Dassault Aviation stock to 300 euros from 210 euros, and kept its hold rating. Shares are up 1.5% at 290.8 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0838 GMT - Bayer's prospects are improving, Berenberg analyst Sebastian Bray says in a note, citing a promising late-stage stroke prevention trial. Although the German pharmaceutical and agricultural conglomerate's late-stage trial for secondary stroke prevention is considered a long shot, there is a possibility of unexpected upside by the year end if the treatment proves significantly better than the placebo, the analyst says. "We believe there is an outside chance of a positive outcome for Bayer that may move the market's view of annual peak sales for the drug from very little to 1 billion euros or more," he says. In addition, if a Russia-Ukraine peace agreement doesn't cause a major drop in soft commodity prices, Bayer's earnings are unlikely to significantly decrease further, the analyst adds. Shares are up 1.7% at 23.65 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0833 GMT - Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index fell 1.85% to 23783.49, weighed down by consumer stocks. Broader risk-taking remains limited, but a short-term rebound is possible, writes Yeap Jun Rong, market strategist at IG. China's inflation data was a significant downside surprise, indicating weak domestic demand beyond the effects of the Lunar New Year holiday. However, China's pledges for proactive fiscal support suggests authorities will address any unexpected economic weakness, Yeap says. Shenzhou International Group fell 5.4%, China Unicom Hong Kong lost 4.9% and Meituan was 4.7% lower. Among advancers, Alibaba Health Information Technology rose 3.25%, Orient Overseas (International) gained 3.0% and Baidu was 1.3% higher. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0829 GMT - Watches of Switzerland's 25 million-pound buyback looks positive in terms of use of funds and timing given that the company's share price is fairly depressed, RBC Capital Markets' Piral Dadhania and Richard Chamberlain say in a note. The U.K. watch retailer likely wants to send a positive message to investors in light of recent share-price weakness, RBC says. "Bearish investors may raise the question as to whether further large scale [merger-and-acquisition activity] is now less likely as a result of this share buyback, which is arguably required for [Watches of Switzerland] to reach its FY28 mid-term revenue target of 3 billion pounds which is not reflected in RBC or consensus expectations currently," the analysts say. Shares rise 4.35% to 470.6 pence, but are down 14% year to date. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0827 GMT - Chemring needs its order intake to accelerate through the rest of this year to meet its guidance, Berenberg analysts warn. They say that a delay in the publication of the U.K. government's Strategic Defense Review, setting out priorities for military spending, could delay orders for the London-listed company, which makes advanced military explosives and high-tech products such as sensors and provides services to organizations including NASA and Elon Musk's SpaceX. Chemring is likely to benefit from higher demand for ammunition from European governments seeking to rearm, they add. But its high level of exposure to the U.S., with 34% of revenue deriving from there, persuaded Berenberg to downgrade Chemring stock to hold from buy, citing the potential for 8% targeted cuts in certain defense programs. Chemring shares are down 0.6% at 4.08 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0754 GMT - German arms makers Rheinmetall, Hensoldt and Renk Group will benefit from the decision agreed by Germany's likely coalition partners to allow defense spending beyond 1% of gross domestic product to be exempted from Germany's current deficit limit, Berenberg analysts say. The center-right CDU, its CSU sister party and the Social Democratic Party also agreed to create a new 500 billion-euro special fund for infrastructure. Berenberg estimates that the German defense budget will increase to 2.6% of GDP by 2028, reaching 111 billion euros by comparison with 52 billion euros in 2024. Hensoldt derives 56% of its revenue from Germany, above the 27% of Renk. In the case of Rheinmetall, 19% of revenue comes from Germany in its defense business. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - The European Central Bank might be too pessimistic about the eurozone's growth chances this year as a planned spending drive looks set to boost activity, Pantheon Macroeconomics' Claus Vistesen and Melanie Debono write in a note to clients. The central bank last week cuts its estimates for output growth in the currency area, pointing notably to the risk of U.S. import tariffs. But those tariffs might not materialise in a sustained fashion, Vistesen and Debono says. And new plans in Germany and across the wider EU to spend more on infrastructure and defense offer a countervailing shot in the arm, they say. "[L]ast week's fiscal announcements point to more balanced risks further out," they say. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0737 GMT - The dollar trades steady but remains at weaker levels after losses last week amid uncertainty over U.S. President Trump's policies. The big issue for markets is the fact that Trump's policies change as "often as the direction of the wind does," Pepperstone strategist Michael Brown says in a note. Trump's whipsawing on tariffs makes it impossible for market participants to price risk or accurately discount a future policy path, he says. There is no surprise that "nobody wants to touch dollar denominated assets right now" with the DXY dollar index suffering its worst week in two-and-a-half years last week, he says. The DXY trades flat at 103.853 after hitting a four-month low of 103.458 Friday.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Eneos Plans $3 Billion IPO of Metals Subsidiary — Update
data.description:
By Kosaku Narioka

Eneos Holdings plans to spin off its metals subsidiary through an initial public offering that is expected to raise nearly $3 billion, the biggest listing in Japan in more than six years.

The Tokyo-based energy giant said Monday that it plans to offer a majority of shares in the wholly owned subsidiary, JX Advanced Metals, in the offering that would raise as much as 438.6 billion yen, equivalent to $2.96 billion. The listing is scheduled for March 19.

Eneos said in February that it would use part of the proceeds to fund investments needed to expand the supply of next-generation energy sources, such as synthetic fuels and hydrogen. No new shares will be issued.

The company has said that JX Advanced Metals, which makes materials used in the semiconductor and information-technology industries, will be able to accelerate capital investments as well as research and development as a listed company.

The Tokyo Stock Exchange hosted several large offerings in recent months after the benchmark Nikkei Stock Average hit record highs last year.

In October, the IPO by subway operator Tokyo Metro raised about $2.31 billion, making it the largest since SoftBank Corp.'s $21 billion IPO in 2018, according to data from Dealogic.

Eneos said it will sell up to 534.9 million shares of JX Advanced Metals, or 57.6% of the total outstanding, at Y820 each, equivalent to $5.54. JX Advanced Metals said earlier this month that it expected to offer shares at around Y815 apiece.

Also on Monday, Japan Post Holdings said it will offer $4 billion of its shares in its banking unit. The postal and financial services giant said a total of about 410 million shares of Japan Post Bank will be offered at Y1,444 each, valuing the offering at about Y592 billion, equivalent to $4 billion.

Write to Kosaku Narioka at kosaku.narioka@wsj.com

data.title: Clarkson Shares Sink as Geopolitical Turbulence Continues to Cause Uncertainty
data.description:
By Anthony O. Goriainoff

Shares in Clarkson plunged after the shipping services company warned of a hit from increased geopolitical uncertainty and said the value of its spot business in the year to date had fallen.

London-listed shares were down 14% at 3,780 pence in morning trade.

Clarkson said it was being hit by the uncertainty stemming from political changes, continuing regional conflicts, increased trade tensions, tariffs and sanctions, inflation as well as changing monetary policy across global economies.

The company said this uncertainty had led to freight rates and asset values broadly falling.

Its spot business includes transactions where goods are shipped and delivered on short notice, without a pre-existing contract.

The company said its forward order book for invoicing in 2025 provided good visibility of baseline future earnings and was $14 million higher as at Dec. 31 at $231 million.

Clarkson said it expected 2025 to be second-half weighted, as has been the case in most years.

"Once the current uncertainty starts to recede, the markets will, over time, rebalance ... and clients will again be able to be more strategic in their forward planning, including a focus on fleet renewal programs," it said.

Write to Anthony O. Goriainoff at anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com

data.title: French Stocks Start the Week Lower
data.description:
The CAC 40 fell 0.5% to 8,080 on Monday, extending losses from the previous session as investors continued to grapple with ongoing economic uncertainties and escalating concerns over global trade tensions.

Adding to the unease, China’s return to deflation has fueled worries about global growth prospects, further compounded by troubling economic data from the U.S. Among individual stocks, Saint-Gobain was the worst performer, with shares plunging 4.3%, followed by Societe Generale, which fell 2.7%, and STMicroelectronics, which declined 2.5%.

On a brighter note, Thales led the index with a 1.1% gain, while Essilor and Eurofins Scientific each advanced around 1%.

data.title: BeiGene Shareholder Hillhouse Pares Stake in Cancer Drug Company
data.description:
BeiGene shareholder Hillhouse Capital reduced their stakes in the cancer drug manufacturer in Hong Kong, according to separate disclosures to the Hong Kong bourse on March 5.

The investment manager, through HHLR Fund and HHLR Advisors, sold 32,240,000 shares at an average price of $18.81 apiece, according to the disclosure.

Hillhouse now holds a 5.70% stake, or 79,357,423 shares, from its 111,597,423 shares, or an 8.01% stake.

data.title: FTSE 100 on Weak Note
data.description:
The FTSE 100 was slightly lower around 8,650 on Monday, as worries about the effect of Trump’s tariffs weighed on financial markets, with fears of a potential U.S. recession also looming.

China's deflation issue is also dampening sentiment, while geopolitical risks persisted.

Defensives are leading the decline, with AstraZeneca down 1.8%, Reckitt Benckiser down 1.75% and BT Group down 1.5% at the bottom of the list. Banks are also in the red, with Barclays down 0.85% and Lloyds down 0.8%.

Defence and aerospace names are seeing some slight profit-taking too after gains in recent weeks, with BAE Systems and Melrose in the red.

data.title: Thales's Space Business Concerns Persist — Market Talk
data.description: Thales's space business, which accounts for 11% of the French aerospace and defense group's revenue, continues to be a source of concern given the structurally weaker commercial telecoms market, Berenberg analysts say. The business generated a 65 million euros loss before interest and taxes in 2024, equivalent to a -3% margin. Thales expects this margin to improve to 7% in 2028. However, Berenberg forecasts the margin will reach just 5% in three years' time. Thales, Airbus and Leonardo are exploring the possibility of bringing together their space units. The best formula would be a joint-venture structure similar to that of the European missile consortium MBDA, which was formed by Airbus, BAE Systems and Leonardo, the Berenberg analysts say. They caution that this outcome seems challenging in the near term. Shares are up 1.4% at 239.9 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Monday at 5 AM ET
data.description:
U.S. stock futures fall after a rough week on Wall Street, as Trump won't rule out recession

U.S. stock futures fell Sunday, as President Donald Trump offered little reassurance after the S&P 500 had its worst week since September.

German Industrial Production Rose at Start of 2025 as Tariff Threats Loom

Output increased 2% on month in January as German industry showed tentative signs of improvement, though that could be threatened by U.S. trade tariffs.

Trump Declines to Rule Out Recession

In an interview that aired Sunday, the president demurred when asked if there were reassurances he could provide to businesses seeking more clarity on tariffs.

Tariff Wars Are Often Short. Their Legacies Aren't.

Economists fear that Trump's tariffs, once they take hold, could have unexpected effects long after he leaves office.

China Struggles to Shake Off Disinflationary Pressure as Trade Threats Loom

Consumer prices nosedived in February, ending a year of tepid price growth.

America's job market is eerily similar to the 1990s dot-com bubble - and, yes, it's a worry

March 20 marks the 25th anniversary of the dot-com bust. Could it happen again?

U.S. Bolsters Position as World's Top Arms Exporter

America has benefited from rising European spending, though President Trump's move to curb support for Ukraine is raising concerns about future purchases.

Stock Investors Go on Defense With Dividends

Some investors are sheltering from market swings in shares of companies with high payouts.

A Government Shutdown Could Create Short-Term Volatility. But It's Mostly Noise.

Tariffs aren't the only concern in Washington DC. A looming government shutdown could temporarily roil the markets too.

Tariffs Inflict Pain on Home Builders When They Can Least Afford It

Trade levies could add an extra $10,000 to the cost of building a home, likely coming out of builders' margins.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Monday at 5 AM ET
data.description:
Foxconn Builds FoxBrain, Its Own AI Model

The world's largest contract electronics maker said that aided by Nvidia it had built a large language model with reasoning capabilities, developed in-house and trained in four weeks.

Is the chip sector having a meltdown? By this measure, it's mostly just Nvidia.

Broadcom and Marvell shares have been hit recently. But Nvidia alone is responsible for about three quarters of the chip sector's lost market cap in the weeks since the DeepSeek news.

AI Profits Are Still a Mystery. An IPO Filing Has Clues.

A new IPO filing from start-up CoreWeave suggests that every dollar put into the AI cloud is quickly paying off thanks to rapid growth rates in the business.

7-Eleven Owner, Couche-Tard Financial Advisers Looking for Potential Buyers for Some Stores

Seven & i Holdings said in a letter to shareholders Monday that Couche-Tard recently agreed to jointly explore a potential sale of some stores prior to signing a takeover agreement.

Bally's Offers Financing Deal to Australia's Star

American betting and entertainment company Bally's has offered to provide embattled casino operator Star Entertainment with additional financing.

Tough to Digest? McDonald's and Yum! Brands Are at Record Highs Despite Consumer Concerns.

The two fast food stocks have surged this year. But can McDonald's and Yum keep satisfying investors' appetite for growth?

CVS's New Mini Stores Go All In on Medicine and Skip Everything Else

The national drugstore chain is preparing to open a dozen stores offering full-service pharmacies but very limited retail.

An Intel deal now seems less likely. These clues may hold the key to its future.

Investors appear to be now be discounting recent M&A speculation that Intel will split in two.

Robinhood to Pay $30 Million for Regulatory Violations, Finra Says

The regulator said Robinhood had violated "numerous" rules, including failing to respond to red flags for potential misconduct.

Activist Mantle Ridge Builds Over $1 Billion Stake in Tech-Services Firm Cognizant

The investor has been working behind the scenes at the tech-services firm and isn't currently planning to nominate directors.

data.title: FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI Index Ends 0.7% Lower at 1536.46
MicroPort Scientific Expects 58% Lower Loss in 2024
data.description:
MicroPort Scientific expects to record a net loss of about $275 million for 2024, a decrease of 58% compared with the same period a year earlier, according to a Friday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

The company attributed the narrowing of loss to the 10% revenue growth despite industry challenges, with overseas business increasing 80% year-on-year. Cost optimization improved efficiency, reducing the operating expense ratio by 28% points year-on-year. Additionally, the successful divestment of non-core businesses contributed positively to the overall financial performance.

The manufacturer and sellers of medical products plan to publish their annual results by the end of March.

The company's shares were down over 5% at the close of trade.

data.title: Market Chatter: Hong Kong Government Confident of Residential Properties Price Stability
data.description:
The Hong Kong government downplayed apprehensions about a glut of residential properties, citing a stable 4.5% private residential homes vacancy rate, with rising rents showing a strong housing demand, The Standard reported Thursday.

The officials believe that with lower interest rates, the inflow of talent, and economic growth, the market will remain stable. The government is taking steps to stabilize the non-residential property prices as well, according to the standard.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Major Gulf markets ease on global trade war concerns
data.description:
Major stock markets in the Gulf fell in early trade on Monday as U.S. import tariffs sparked concerns over global economic growth, causing investors to shy away from riskier assets.

U.S. President Donald Trump, in a Fox News interview on Sunday, declined to predict whether his tariffs on China, Canada and Mexico would result in a U.S. recession.

Tariffs have been a key concern for investors, with many believing that they can hurt economic growth and be inflationary.

Saudi Arabia's benchmark index dropped 0.6%, hit by a 2.1% fall in ACWA Power Company and a 2.2% decrease in Saudi Arabian Mining Company .

Elsewhere, oil giant Saudi Aramco was down 0.6%.

Oil prices - a catalyst for the Gulf's financial markets - fell on concerns over U.S. tariffs' impact on global growth and rising OPEC+ output.

However, Derayah Financial (4084.SE) surged 30% in debut trade - the maximum daily limit allowed by Tadawul for newly listed stocks during their first three days of trading.

Dubai's main share index retreated 0.7%, with blue-chip developer Emaar Properties losing 1.1% and toll operator Salik declining 1.8%.

In Abu Dhabi, the index eased 0.1%.

A run of soft U.S. economic data continued on Friday after monthly figures showed the labour market created fewer jobs than expected in February, in the first payrolls report capturing Trump's policies.

Investors now await the U.S. Consumer Price Index (CPI) data due on Wednesday and Producer Price Index (PPI) data expected on Thursday, to gauge the Federal Reserve's interest rate stance.

Monetary policy in the six-member Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) is usually guided by the Fed's decisions, as most regional currencies are pegged to the dollar.

The Qatari index fell 0.4%, with the Gulf's biggest lender Qatar National Bank dropping 0.3%.

data.title: Heard on the Street Friday Recap: Feeling Fine — WSJ
data.description:
By Heard Editors

What Happened in Markets Friday:

The jobs report fell short of expectations. The U.S. economy added 151,000 jobs in February, shy of the 170,000 forecast by economists. The unemployment rate ticked up to 4.1%, from 4%.

Stocks rose nonetheless. Perhaps investors were expecting something worse. The S&P 500 added 0.6%, while the Nasdaq Composite rose 0.7%. The Dow Jones Industrial Average gained 222 points.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said the economy was "fine," and the central bank is content to wait for more clarity on the outlook before making any interest-rate moves.

Treasury yields rose. Two-year U.S. Treasury yields reached 4%, helping snap a three-week streak of falling yields. Investors may fell less confident the Fed remains on track to cut rates.

Costco missed Wall Street revenue expectations. The warehouse retailer's shares fell 6% following its quarterly earnings. The company said it wasn't seeing any big changes in members' spending habits.

Broadcom soothed AI slowdown fears. The chipmaker's earnings report topped forecasts, and the company said it was working with additional data-center operators. Broadcom shares rallied more than 8%.

  • Check out WSJ Minute Briefing's podcast of the day in markets, and read up on stock-movers of the week in The Score column.

This analysis comes from the Journal's Heard on the Street team. Subscribe to their free daily afternoon newsletter here.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Sweden's Riksbank says it needs to be possible to pay offline with cards
data.description:
Sweden's central bank said on Monday that, to strengthen resilience in the Nordic country's payment system in light of geopolitical unease, it urgently needs to be possible to pay offline with cards if the internet is not working.

"In light of the deteriorating security situation in Sweden and our neighbouring region, both public and private actors need to urgently step up their efforts to create a payments market that can withstand disruptions," the Riksbank said.

"We have brought together the relevant stakeholders and together we will have a solution in place by 1 July 2026," it said in a summary of an annual report on the retail payments system.
